title: Licence to Chill: Launching the 100 Contracts Study
content:
Blog post by Ana Lazarova

Libraries are essential infrastructures for research, education, culture, and lifelong learning. Every day, academic, public, research, and school libraries connect people with knowledge, preserve cultural heritage, and support innovation. To fulfil these missions, libraries rely on legal rights that allow them to use copyrighted materials in the public interest.

Knowledge Rights 21 is pleased to launch the 100 Contracts Study, a research project examining how licensing agreements affect libraries’ ability to exercise those rights.

Inspired by a 2008 British Library analysis of licensing agreements, this study, conducted between February and August 2023, evaluates 100 contracts between publishers and academic and public libraries across 14 Council of Europe countries. It draws on a dataset of licensing agreements sourced from libraries within the KR21 and IFLA networks, as well as information consortia, and publicly available publisher terms.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Preventing Contract Override: Recommendations for Libraries and Legislators
content:
Blog post by Benjamin White

A Study of 100 Contracts, published by Knowledge Rights 21, shines a light on the critical issue that contracts severely undermine the ability of  libraries to deliver access to knowledge to support research, education and cultural participation.

With the move from physical ownership to digital licensing, what libraries and users can do is now largely defined by the terms of contracts negotiated with rightsholders.

One major issue for libraries is that there often is not the resource to negotiate every licence. 

Even where resources are available, agreements frequently constitute contracts of adhesion, in which libraries have little, if any, scope to negotiate the contract in any meaningful sense. 

As a result, contractual arrangements can restrict or prohibit certain uses of content that are otherwise allowed by copyright law. 

The override of permitted uses in this way severely threatens and disrupts a library’s work by diluting or removing long-held legally established rights to copy and share content.
creator: KR21 Guest
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: The Digital Omnibus on Data Must Work for Research Too
content:
The Digital Omnibus on Data, the first major piece of legislation to reach the European Parliament and Council in the 2024-2029 mandate, is being built largely around the needs of business.

That leaves two gaps for research and libraries: a definition of ‘research data’ that does not yet cover the metadata needed to find and assess research, and equal protection against unfair contract terms for research institutions seeking to access data. Knowledge Rights 21 (KR21) is calling on the European Parliament and Council to close both gaps as the file moves forward.

The Omnibus itself is branded as a simplification exercise, merging the Data Governance Act, the Open Data Directive, and the Data Act, and amending the General Data Protection Regulation, to give data-intensive sectors a single rule-book to follow.

One of the pre-existing pieces of legislation to be absorbed is the Open Data Directive, agreed in 2019 to update the EU’s rules on public sector information.
snippet: The Digital Omnibus on Data, the first major piece of legislation to reach the European Parliament and Council in the 2024-2029 mandate, is being built largely around the needs of business.
creator: Stephen Wyber
sourceDescription: We advocate for your right to access and share knowledge!

title: Welcoming the Irish EU Presidency
content:
Throughout the second half of 2026, Irish representatives will take charge of the vast majority of meetings of the European Union’s Member States. As usual, this presidency will be about advancing ongoing legislative agendas, but also offer opportunities to highlight particular national priorities and themes. 

Over the next weeks, there will be lots of presentations of forward plans across different European Parliament committees as well as in different formations of the Council of Ministers. This article highlights those that are particularly relevant in Knowledge Rights 21’s areas of work, and some questions we hope we’ll see answered. 

Maintaining the Legislative Momentum

The Presidency’s overall themes align well with the EU’s wider agenda at the moment, with a focus on competitiveness, values and security. Under the competitiveness pillar, it offers five building blocks – simplification, an integrated single market, championing trade, energy transition and digital and AI. All, again, are close to ongoing priorities.
